original_abstract: Abstract The rise of neurotechnologies, especially in combination with artificial intelligence (AI)-based methods for brain data analytics, has given rise to concerns around the protection of mental privacy, mental integrity and cognitive liberty – often framed as “neurorights” in ethical, legal, and policy discussions. Several states are now looking at including neurorights into their constitutional legal frameworks, and international institutions and organizations, such as UNESCO and the Council of Europe, are taking an active interest in developing international policy and governance guidelines on this issue. However, in many discussions of neurorights the philosophical assumptions, ethical frames of reference and legal interpretation are either not made explicit or conflict with each other. The aim of this multidisciplinary work is to provide conceptual, ethical, and legal foundations that allow for facilitating a common minimalist conceptual understanding of mental privacy, mental integrity, and cognitive liberty to facilitate scholarly, legal, and policy discussions.
humanized_abstract: Chapter 1: Introduction Neurotechnologies and the need for neurorights Understanding the scope of ethical, legal and policy debates around neurorights has become increasingly important. These issues have emerged with respect to the rapidly evolving neurotechnology field, especially when combined with the increasingly popular brain-computer interface use of data analysis techniques from the artificial intelligence (AI) field. Such developments present a risk to fundamental human rights including mental privacy, mental integrity and cognitive liberty – rights to which the term “neurorights” is now increasingly applied in the context of ethics, law and governance. As things currently stand, a number of states are considering including neurorights in their national Constitutions. Internationally, for example, UNESCO and the Council of Europe are also increasingly focussing on developing a formal policy response. Yet, one of the problems of the neurorights literature in ethics, law and governance is that the underlying conceptual, ethical and legal frameworks are often unclear, implied or even conflicting – with different concepts being used without sufficient clarification of their meaning. This interdisciplinary volume has therefore sought to meet the need for providing foundational thinking for scholars, lawyers and policy-makers. It focusses on the pursuit of an agreed, minimalist, set of common definitions of mental privacy, mental integrity and cognitive liberty upon which scholarship, law and policy can be developed.
